cards.text: A {{c1::function try block}} is a try block that encompasses the entire function body, including the {{c2::member initializer list}} for constructors.
In a function try block, the {{c1::try}} keyword is placed before the function body (or member initializer list), and {{c2::catch}} blocks follow the closing brace of the function.

cards.question: What does the try keyword cover here?
cards.answer: The try covers both the member initializer list (m_x, m_y) and the constructor body.
cards.explanation: Function try blocks allow catching exceptions from member initialization, which regular try blocks inside the constructor cannot do.

cards.front: What is the primary use case for function try blocks?
cards.back: Catching exceptions thrown from member initializer lists in constructors, which cannot be caught by regular try blocks inside the constructor body.

cards.question: Is this a valid function try block?
cards.answer: Yes, this is valid. Function try blocks can be used with any function, not just constructors.
cards.explanation: While primarily useful for constructors, function try blocks can be used with any function to wrap the entire function body.

cards.text: When a constructor function try block catch handler finishes, the exception is {{c1::automatically rethrown}}. You cannot prevent the exception from propagating by {{c2::not rethrowing}}.

cards.question: Does this catch block prevent the exception from propagating?
cards.answer: No, the exception is automatically rethrown after the catch block.
cards.explanation: Constructor function try block catch handlers always rethrow the exception implicitly, even without an explicit throw; statement.

cards.front: Do function try blocks for non-constructor functions automatically rethrow exceptions?
cards.back: No, only constructor function try blocks automatically rethrow. Regular function try blocks behave like normal catch blocks.

cards.concept: Function try blocks and constructor exception handling
cards.explanation:
Function try blocks solve a unique problem with constructors:
  • Member initializer lists run before constructor body
  • Regular try blocks can't catch initializer exceptions
  • Function try blocks cover the entire constructor
  • Catch handlers always rethrow (object construction failed)
  • Mainly used for logging or cleanup before rethrow

cards.front: Can a regular try-catch block inside a constructor body catch exceptions from the member initializer list?
cards.back: No, because the member initializer list executes before the constructor body, so try blocks inside the body cannot catch those exceptions.

cards.text: Using function try blocks with destructors is problematic because if a destructor throws during stack unwinding, {{c1::std::terminate()}} is called. Destructors should {{c2::not throw}} exceptions.

cards.front: Why are function try blocks rarely used in practice?
cards.back:
They're rarely needed because:
  • Most functions don't need to catch all exceptions
  • For constructors, they can only log/cleanup before automatic rethrow
  • Member initialization usually shouldn't throw
  • Regular try blocks are more flexible
